summary: DeVault argues that the fundamental bargain of open source software is surrendering your monopoly over commercial exploitation. Companies struggling to understand FOSS philosophy must accept that anyone—competitors, cloud providers, consultants—can monetize their code. He frames this as karmic justice: if the community contributes value beyond what any single organization could create, the commercial benefits shouldn't be captured by that organization alone. The GPL family of licenses offers a middle ground by requiring improvements be shared back. DeVault concludes that FOSS creates socialized software wealth rather than monopolies.
key_points: FOSS licenses exist for the express purpose of breaking the artificial monopoly that copyright laws create for businesses
If the community adds value beyond what your organization created alone, it's fair that commercial exploitation isn't exclusively yours either
Building a business on FOSS requires more than writing the software—you must figure out how to capture value in a non-monopolistic ecosystem
GPL licenses offer a mechanism to ensure others who profit must also share their improvements back to the community
Both producers and consumers of FOSS must understand and honor license obligations—it's not a free lunch
money_quotes: Participation in open source requires you to surrender your monopoly over commercial exploitation.
Anyone can monetize your code. That includes you, and me, all of your contributors, your competitors, Amazon and Google, and everyone else.
Copyright laws exist for the express purpose of creating an artificial monopoly for your business, and FOSS licenses exist for the express purpose of breaking it.
It will create wealth for everyone, not just the original authors. And if you want it to create wealth for you, you are responsible for figuring out how.

If the sum of its value is no longer entirely accountable to your organization, is it not fair that the commercial exploitation of that value shouldn't be entirely captured by your organization, either?
Capitalism concerns itself with making monopolies — FOSS instead concerns itself with the socialized creation of software wealth.
Building a business requires more work than just writing the software.
key_insight: Open source is fundamentally a rejection of commercial monopoly—if you accept community contributions, you must accept that anyone can profit from the result.
